senses.gloss: [with noun or pronoun ‘how is it different’, along with مِن (min, + noun or pronoun) ‘from something else’] (usually implying there is no relationship)

examples.english: What does this have to do with that? / What comparison is there between them?!
How far is the soil from the Pleiades!

examples.text: الحملات العامة تجلب الناس المحاذين للخطر إلى مراكز مثل مركز المراقبة بي.سي.أن ، أين^([sic]) يمكنهم إجراء إختبار^([sic]) على فيروس فقدان المناعة بطريقة سريعة ومجهولة

etymology.text:
From the root ء ي ن (ʔ y n), meaning “to come or approach”, “to become or happen”, “to draw near to a place or time”; related to ء ي ي (ʔ y y) and آن (ʔān).

Causative based on the root ء ي ن (ʔ y n) extracted from أَيُون (ʔayūn, “ion”).

body.text: (S, K, &c.) [Whether it be a simple subst., or an inf. n., and, if the latter, whether it be an inf. n. of
only in the former of the two senses assigned to that verb above, or in both these senses, is doubted: see 1, throughout.]       -A2-
is an adverbial noun, (Msb,) an interrogative respecting a place: (S, M, Msb, K:) [signifying
]: Zj says that it is an interrogative particle, like
: (T:) [ISd says,] it is a noun, because you say,
]: it is always mansoob, unless you prefix the article to it, saying
]: (Lth, T:) it is fem.; but may be made masc. (Lh, M.) You say,
whither, wilt thou be taken away, and what will be done with thee and made to come to pass with thee,
if this be thine intellect? or, accord. to Mtr, it is a saying of the people of Baghdád, addressed to him whom they charge with foolish judgment or opinion, as meaning
whither, is thine intellect taken away?
What place does this hold in relation to that,
in comparison with that? what is this in relation to that,
in comparison with that? what has this to do with that? what has this in common with that?
] ―         -b2-  It also denotes a condition: when you say,
Where thou sittest, I will sit
], the sitting must be in one place: and
is added to it; so that you say,
Wherever thou standest, I will stand
]. (Msb.) ―         -b3-  It also occurs used as a proper name of a particular place: thus the poet Homeyd Ibn-Thowr speaks of his companions as being
In certain places: where and wherever those places were,
there were my companions]: in which case it is divested of the meaning of an interrogative, and is imperfectly decl. because determinate and of the fem. gender. (M, L. [In one copy of the former,
